statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: The input consists of n vertices, represented as numbers from 0 to n - 1. The first line contains the number of edges. The following lines describe edges as pairs of vertices.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether a simple cycle exists, and if so, the length of the shortest cycle.

examples.explanation: There is a simple cycle of length 2

hints.text: A simple cycle is a Hamiltonian cycle.

editorial.title: Editorial: Shortest Simple Cycle
editorial.intuition: Use a depth-first search to find a Hamiltonian cycle. If such a cycle exists, return its length. Otherwise, return -1.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute-force all permutations of the vertices and check if each permutation forms a Hamiltonian cycle.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a DFS to find a Hamiltonian cycle and a disjoint-set data structure to detect if the cycle contains any self-loops or multiple edges between two vertices.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a DFS to find a Hamiltonian cycle and a disjoint-set data structure to detect if the cycle contains any self-loops or multiple edges between two vertices. If such a cycle exists, return its length. Otherwise, return -1.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution correctly returns the length of the shortest Hamiltonian cycle if one exists, and -1 otherwise.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the algorithm.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle multiple edges between two vertices correctly.
